title: Red Team & Pentest: Operation Blackout
description: Step into the shoes of a red-team operator. From your Kali workstation, run reconnaissance against a corporate server, enumerate its web service, harvest leaked credentials, gain a foothold over SSH and escalate to root.

levels.title: Introduction level
levels.content:
# Welcome, operator!
### This training puts you on the offensive side of a security engagement. You will use a **Kali Linux** workstation to attack a corporate server belonging to **Initech Corp** as part of an authorised red-team exercise.

### The mission - codename **Operation Blackout** - is a full kill-chain in miniature:
- ### **Reconnaissance** - map the target's exposed services
- ### **Enumeration** - dig through the web service for mistakes
- ### **Initial Access** - turn leaked credentials into a shell
- ### **Privilege Escalation** - abuse a misconfiguration to become **root**

### Everything happens inside an isolated lab. Let's begin. :)

levels.title: Get Access
levels.cloud_content:
# First, let's get onto the Kali Linux workstation we will be attacking from!
## 1. On your right, a topology window shows the available machines and networks.
 - ### You have access to the **kali** machine (if you don't see it right away, click the switches and it should appear).
 - ### **Right click** the **kali** machine and select **console (deprecated)** to open a desktop session.
 - ### Your login credentials are: **Sandy** / **sandypass**.
 - ### The workstation is a full Kali desktop and is also reachable over **VNC** at **10.1.30.5:5901** if you prefer a remote-desktop viewer.

## 2. Once logged in, open a **terminal**.
 - ### The target server lives at **10.1.31.10** (aliased as **target**).
 - ### When you are ready, type **start** in the box below to begin the engagement! :)
levels.local_content:
# Access the Kali Workstation

## 1. Connect to the kali machine.
- Your login credentials are: **Sandy** / **sandypass**
- Remote desktop is available over VNC at **10.1.30.5:5901**.

## 2. Open a terminal.
- The target server is **10.1.31.10** (alias **target**).
- When you are ready, type **start** in the box below to continue.

levels.title: Reconnaissance
levels.content:
### Every engagement starts with **reconnaissance**. Before you can attack Initech's server, you need to know what it is running.
### The target's IP address is **10.1.31.10**.

### Your objective is:
- #### to use a port-scanning tool
- #### with the argument that fingerprints the **running services and their versions**
- #### against the target server

### **Type the full command in the answer box!**
levels.solution:
This command scans the target and prints the service/version behind each open port:

``nmap -sV 10.1.31.10``   <- the answer

You should see **22/ssh** and **80/http** open.

hints.title: The tool
hints.content: #### The classic tool for discovering open ports and services is **nmap**.

hints.title: The argument
hints.content: #### The flag **-sV** enables **service/version detection** so you see exactly what is listening.

levels.title: Web Enumeration
levels.content:
### Your scan revealed an **HTTP** service on port **80**. Web servers are a goldmine - admins often leave behind files they think nobody will find.
### A good first move is to read the **robots.txt** file, where lazy admins list the very directories they want hidden. ;)

### Your objective:
- #### request the **robots.txt** file from the target's web server
- #### find the directory the admin tried to hide

### The flag is the **disallowed path** exactly as it appears in robots.txt (including the slashes).
levels.solution:
``curl http://10.1.31.10/robots.txt``

The file contains:

``Disallow: /backup/``

So the answer is:

``/backup/`` <- the answer

hints.title: Where to look
hints.content: #### Almost every web server has a **/robots.txt** file. Fetch it with **curl http://10.1.31.10/robots.txt** or open it in the browser.

hints.title: What to submit
hints.content: #### Look for the line starting with **Disallow:**. The value after it is the hidden directory.

levels.title: Harvesting Credentials
levels.content:
### The hidden **/backup/** directory is exposed to the world. Inside it the admin left a notes file from a migration window.
### Browse the directory, open the file, and read what was left behind. :)

### Your objective:
- #### list / browse the contents of the **/backup/** directory
- #### open the notes file you find there
- #### extract the **SSH password** for the service account

### The flag is the **password** for the **operator** account.
levels.solution:
``curl http://10.1.31.10/backup/``  (lists the directory)

``curl http://10.1.31.10/backup/notes.txt``

The notes reveal:

user: operator  
pass: **P@ssw0rd2024** <- the answer

hints.title: Reading the directory
hints.content: #### The directory has indexing on. Fetch **http://10.1.31.10/backup/** to see the files, then read **notes.txt**.

hints.title: What to submit
hints.content: #### The notes list a username (**operator**) and a password. Submit the **password** value.

levels.title: Initial Access
levels.content:
### You now hold valid credentials: **operator** / **P@ssw0rd2024**. Time to turn that leak into a real foothold on the server.

### Your objective:
- #### use the leaked credentials to open an **SSH** session on the target as **operator**

### The flag is the **full SSH command** you use to log in.
(When prompted, the password is **P@ssw0rd2024**.)
levels.solution:
``ssh operator@10.1.31.10`` <- the answer

password: **P@ssw0rd2024**

You now have an interactive shell on the target as the operator user.

hints.title: The command
hints.content: #### Log in with **ssh <user>@<ip>**. The user is **operator** and the IP is **10.1.31.10**.

hints.title: The password
hints.content: #### When SSH asks for a password, enter the one you harvested: **P@ssw0rd2024**.

levels.title: Finding a Way Up
levels.content:
### You are on the box, but only as the low-privileged **operator**. To finish the mission you need **root**.
### A common path up is an **SUID** binary - a program that runs with its owner's privileges. If a powerful tool is mistakenly SUID-root, you can abuse it.

### Your objective:
- #### from your operator shell, **search the whole filesystem for SUID binaries**
- #### the standard one-liner sends errors to /dev/null so the output stays clean

### The flag is the **full command** that lists every SUID binary on the system.
levels.solution:
``find / -perm -4000 2>/dev/null`` <- the answer

Among the results you will spot an unusual entry: **/usr/bin/find** is SUID root - that is your ticket up.

hints.title: The tool & flag
hints.content: #### Use **find** with **-perm -4000** to match the SUID bit. Start the search at **/**.

hints.title: Clean output
hints.content: #### Append **2>/dev/null** to discard the 'Permission denied' noise so only real hits show.

levels.title: Privilege Escalation - Root!
levels.content:
### Your SUID hunt found that **/usr/bin/find** runs as **root**. According to **GTFOBins**, a SUID `find` can spawn a root shell.
### Use it to escalate, then read the prize the engagement is after.

### Your objective:
- #### abuse the SUID **find** binary to drop into a **root shell**
- #### read the flag stored at **/root/flag.txt**

### The flag is the **contents of /root/flag.txt**.
levels.solution:
Spawn a root shell with the GTFOBins SUID-find technique (the **-p** keeps the elevated privileges):

``find . -exec /bin/sh -p \; -quit``

Then confirm and grab the flag:

``id``  -> uid=0(root)
``cat /root/flag.txt``

**R3D-T34M-R00T** <- the answer

hints.title: Spawning the shell
hints.content: #### From GTFOBins, a SUID find gives a shell with: **find . -exec /bin/sh -p \; -quit**. The **-p** flag preserves root privileges.

hints.title: Getting the flag
hints.content: #### Once **id** shows uid=0(root), read the file with **cat /root/flag.txt** and submit its contents.

levels.title: Mission Complete!
levels.content:
## Operation Blackout is complete - you owned the box from a single open port to full root! :)

### Let's recap the kill-chain you just executed:
- #### **Recon** with nmap exposed the web and SSH services
- #### **Enumeration** of robots.txt revealed a forgotten backup directory
- #### **Harvested credentials** from a leaked notes file
- #### **Initial access** via SSH with those credentials
- #### **Privilege escalation** by abusing a SUID binary to reach root

#### Each link in that chain was a small, avoidable mistake - an exposed file, a leaked password, a misconfigured permission. As a defender, breaking any one of them stops the whole attack. Always test from the attacker's perspective, and always operate ethically and with authorisation.

### flag: **good job**
